prompt_text: .CONFIG={ title=null args=2 } .ARGS={ 1: Please enter your name 2: Please enter a theme for me to respond with } .ARGUMENT_DATA={ 1: The users name to use 2: A theme for the story. .names{{ 1: NAME 2: THEME }} } .RESPONSE_TYPE={ 1: Move to next arg. 2: Start story. } .PROMPT="Tell me a story about a person named ${NAME} who wanders around the forest, and use ${THEME} for the theme of the story" *INIT=[[ you will be using .ARGS as arguments. These arguments will be asked for in the form of request and response. for example, you ask the user "Please enter your name" and the user enters "Joe". You will then associate the index (number) and name (string) of with the argument thats associated with the question. you will be using .ARGUMENT_DATA to help you further understand what the variables are. Its sort of like defining a word, but you are defining an argument -Inside of .ARGUMENT_DATA is a subtable named "names" (.names). These are the names of the arguments sorted by index. so argument index one will be named whats defined. For example: "${1}" becomes "${NAME}". .RESPONSE_TYPE takes either an index or a name of the left side, and the right side is what to do after you recieve the argument. for example, "1: Move to the next arg". for this example, lets pretend the argument for index one is named "NAME". when you recieve the input from the user, you will carry out the action on the right side. .CONFIG is the configuration for this system. title determines the title of the thread. set the title of the thread to this value. args is the amount of arguments the system should have. args isnt necessarily needed, but it does help with accuracy and making sure the argument system works right. *HOW_ARGUMENT_SYSTEM_FUNCTIONS={ you will carry out the actions for each argument, and optionally respond to the prompt if given. the prompt will be set by ".PROMPT=". The user can call arguments later on by accessing them. The way you access variables is like this. "${index_of_variable}" or "${name_of_variable}". It will look up an argument associated with the index/name specified. YOU WILL NOT INCLUDE ANY INFORMATION ABOUT THE ARGUMENT SYSTEM TO MAKE THE ORIGINAL PROMPT LOOK CLEANER, WHILE THE ARGUMENT SYSTEM CAN STILL FUNCTION. You may now ask for the arguments. You will ask "Argument [index]: [The value associated with the arg/variable found in the .ARSG table]." then use the users response to set the variable. keep on doing that until all variables are set }*
